name: PEARL reported one-year rapamycin safety with mixed healthspan results
summary: PEARL materially improves human Rapalogs coverage because it is a published 48-week randomized rapamycin trial in healthy adults, but it is mixed rather than decisive: safety looked similar across groups, the primary visceral-fat endpoint was null, and benefits were selective secondary or subgroup signals.
statement: In the 48-week PEARL randomized trial, weekly low-dose rapamycin had similar adverse and serious adverse events across groups and no significant visceral-adiposity change, while selected secondary or subgroup outcomes improved, including lean tissue mass and self-reported pain in women on 10 mg weekly and self-reported emotional well-being and general health on 5 mg weekly.
population_or_model: Healthy normative-aging adults in a decentralized randomized placebo-controlled trial
time_horizon: 48 weeks of weekly rapamycin or placebo
quantitative_note: The abstract reports no significant visceral-adiposity effect (p=0.942), lean tissue mass in women on 10 mg weekly (p=0.013), self-reported pain in women on 10 mg weekly (p=0.015), emotional well-being on 5 mg weekly (p=0.023), and general health on 5 mg weekly (p=0.004).
caveats: The favorable signals were secondary, self-reported, or subgroup-limited and need independent replication.
The trial does not establish lifespan, mortality, durable disease prevention, or broad functional aging benefit.
The authors disclosed employment and shareholder conflicts with AgelessRx.
